name: Izanagi's Journey to Yomi
shortDescription: Izanagi's Journey to Yomi is a Japanese myth detailing the Shinto deity Izanagi's descent into the underworld to retrieve his deceased wife, Izanami, and his subsequent defilement and purification.
description: Izanagi's Journey to Yomi is a core narrative in Shinto mythology, representing themes of death, purification, and the separation of the realms of the living and the dead. After the death of his wife and sister, Izanami, during the birthing of the fire deity Kagutsuchi, Izanagi descends into Yomi, the land of the dead, to bring her back. He finds her in a decaying state and is forbidden from looking at her. However, his love and grief compel him to break this taboo. Horrified by her rotting form, Izanagi flees, pursued by Izanami and the demons of Yomi. He seals the entrance to Yomi with a large boulder, effectively separating the world of the living from the world of the dead. This act leads to Izanami vowing to kill a thousand people each day, to which Izanagi retorts that he will create fifteen hundred. Upon returning to the world of the living, Izanagi performs a ritual of purification in a river, giving rise to several important deities, including Amaterasu (sun goddess), Tsukuyomi (moon god), and Susanoo (storm god). This myth explores the nature of death, the consequences of breaking taboos, and the necessity of purification. It also highlights the dynamic and often adversarial relationship between the divine couple, Izanagi and Izanami, and their role in shaping the cosmos. The journey to Yomi is often compared to other mythological journeys to the underworld, such as Orpheus's attempt to retrieve Eurydice or Inanna's descent into Kur. It serves as a potent symbol of the human struggle against mortality and the desire to overcome loss. The themes of defilement and purification are also central to Shinto beliefs and practices.
